description:
You are acting as a DBA who has to dig through a query-performance log, generate a slow-query report, and tweak a PostgreSQL configuration file – all directly from a Linux terminal using nothing but standard text–processing utilities (awk, sed, etc.).  
  The work must be done inside the normal user home directory tree ( /home/user ); you do not have root.  
  Everything you create or touch must remain in place after your session and *exact* output/formatting matters because automated tests will check it byte-for-byte.

  1. Starting situation  
     a. Log directory: /home/user/db_logs  
        • File: /home/user/db_logs/query_performance.log (already exists)  
     b. Config directory: /home/user/db_config  
        • File: /home/user/db_config/postgres.conf (already exists)  
     c. Report directory does *not* exist yet: you must create /home/user/db_reports  

  2. Goal – produce two artefacts from the log file  
     2.1 CSV report: /home/user/db_reports/slow_queries_report.csv  
        • First line is a header, spelled *exactly*:  
          timestamp,query_id,duration_ms,query_text  
        • One subsequent line per “slow” query (any query whose duration value is **1000 ms or greater**).  
        • Each field must be extracted as follows:  
          - timestamp  : the full timestamp inside the square brackets, without the brackets.  
          - query_id   : the numeric value that follows “QUERY_ID=”.  
          - duration_ms: the numeric portion of the “duration=” token, without the “ms” suffix.  
          - query_text : every character from the first SQL keyword up to, but not including, the trailing newline (preserve internal spaces).  
        • There should be *no* extra whitespace around commas or at line ends.  
        • Lines must be ordered in the same order they occur in the original log.  
        • Use awk and/or sed only (no python, perl, etc.).  

     2.2 Summary log: /home/user/db_reports/slow_queries_summary.log  
        • Single-line file containing only:  
          TOTAL_SLOW_QUERIES=<integer>  
        • The integer is the exact count of slow queries written to the CSV.  
        • The file must end with an ordinary UNIX newline (LF).  

  3. Goal – modify a PostgreSQL configuration  
     You must *in-place* edit /home/user/db_config/postgres.conf with sed to achieve both of the following changes:  
       a. Change the value of work_mem from 4MB to 8MB (keep spacing exactly “work_mem = 8MB”).  
       b. Comment out the entire line that reads “enable_seqscan = off” by prefixing it with a single hash (#) and one space, i.e.  
          “# enable_seqscan = off”  
          – The original spacing of “enable_seqscan = off” after the comment marker must stay the same.  
     Additionally, create an unmodified backup copy named /home/user/db_config/postgres.conf.bak **before** you touch the file.  
     Only standard sed is allowed for the in-place edit; do not open the file in an editor.  

  4. Verification hints (what the grader will look for)  
     a. Directory /home/user/db_reports exists and contains exactly the two requested files, nothing else.  
     b. slow_queries_report.csv has:  
        • Exactly three lines for the supplied starting data: 1 header + 2 slow-query rows.  
        • Comma-separated values with no surrounding spaces.  
        • A terminating newline on the last line.  
     c. slow_queries_summary.log contains precisely “TOTAL_SLOW_QUERIES=2\n”.  
     d. /home/user/db_config/postgres.conf.bak is an unmodified byte-for-byte copy of the original postgres.conf.  
     e. The active postgres.conf shows the changed work_mem line and the newly commented enable_seqscan line, and nothing else is altered.  

  5. Allowed toolbox  
     Restricted to core GNU utilities (cat, grep, awk, sed, cut, sort, head, tail, etc.).  
     Do *not* compile code, install packages, run psql, or require root privileges.  

  Execute the full workflow in roughly 5–10 shell commands.  Once done, double-check file paths, spelling, and exact whitespace because the grader is unforgiving.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth files and their exact initial / expected contents -->

  <!-- Initial log file that already exists at task start -->
  <file path="/home/user/db_logs/query_performance.log"><![CDATA[
[2023-06-18 09:00:01] QUERY_ID=1001 duration=250ms select * from accounts where id=1;
[2023-06-18 09:00:05] QUERY_ID=1002 duration=1200ms select * from transactions where amount > 1000;
[2023-06-18 09:00:10] QUERY_ID=1003 duration=810ms update accounts set balance=balance+100 where id=2;
[2023-06-18 09:00:20] QUERY_ID=1004 duration=1350ms delete from sessions where expired=1;
[2023-06-18 09:00:25] QUERY_ID=1005 duration=90ms select * from accounts;
]]></file>

  <!-- Initial configuration file that already exists at task start -->
  <file path="/home/user/db_config/postgres.conf"><![CDATA[
# Database configuration
work_mem = 4MB
enable_seqscan = off
shared_buffers = 128MB
]]></file>

  <!-- Expected CSV report after agent completes the task -->
  <expected_file path="/home/user/db_reports/slow_queries_report.csv"><![CDATA[
timestamp,query_id,duration_ms,query_text
2023-06-18 09:00:05,1002,1200,select * from transactions where amount > 1000;
2023-06-18 09:00:20,1004,1350,delete from sessions where expired=1;
]]></expected_file>

  <!-- Expected summary log -->
  <expected_file path="/home/user/db_reports/slow_queries_summary.log"><![CDATA[
TOTAL_SLOW_QUERIES=2
]]></expected_file>

  <!-- Expected modified configuration file -->
  <expected_file path="/home/user/db_config/postgres.conf"><![CDATA[
# Database configuration
work_mem = 8MB
# enable_seqscan = off
shared_buffers = 128MB
]]></expected_file>

  <!-- Expected unmodified backup -->
  <expected_backup path="/home/user/db_config/postgres.conf.bak"><![CDATA[
# Database configuration
work_mem = 4MB
enable_seqscan = off
shared_buffers = 128MB
]]></expected_backup>
